title: Ridge in Practice
concept: Ridge regression reduces variance and stabilizes predictions, especially when features are highly correlated (multicollinearity).

exercises.prompt: Recap: what makes Ridge regression different from feature selection methods?

options.text: It shrinks weights close to zero but keeps all features in the model
It deletes half the features automatically
It makes the model weights get larger over time

exercises.explain: Right! Ridge shrinks weights but keeps all features. Now, let's look at how Ridge performs in real-world scenarios, particularly with highly correlated features.

cards.title: The copycat columns
cards.body: Imagine predicting home prices. Your dataset has both square footage and square meters. They measure the exact same thing! This copycat data makes standard regression panic and swing its weights wildly.

cards.title: Solving Multicollinearity
cards.body: Multicollinearity happens when features are highly correlated. Standard regression struggles to distribute weight between them. Ridge solves this by sharing the weight smoothly and keeping them stable.

cards.title: Sharing the load
cards.body: Imagine two workers lifting a heavy box. Standard regression might tell one to lift with 1000 lbs of force and the other to pull down with 990 lbs. Ridge steps in and says: 'Just lift 5 lbs each.' It shares the load safely.

cards.title: Stable real estate predictions
cards.body: In real estate, number of bathrooms and total rooms are highly correlated. Ridge prevents the model from giving one a giant positive weight and the other a giant negative weight, keeping predictions stable.

cards.title: Does Ridge always win?
cards.body: Ridge reduces variance (overfitting) by shrinking weights, but it does increase bias (underfitting) slightly. It is a trade-off, but one that almost always leads to better real-world predictions.

cards.title: Correlated Features: Standard vs Ridge
cards.body: How the two models distribute weights for identical features:

options.text: When independent features are highly correlated with each other
When the target variable is negative
When the model runs out of training data
When there is only one feature in the model

exercises.explain: Multicollinearity describes features that move together in a strong pattern, which confuses standard linear regression.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Ridge regression reduces the variance (overfitting) of a model at the cost of slightly increasing its bias (underfitting).
exercises.explain: True! Regularization is a trade-off. We accept a tiny bit of bias to get a massive drop in variance, leading to better predictions on new data.

exercises.sentence: Ridge regression helps stabilize models when features are highly ___.
exercises.explain: Highly correlated features can cause standard regression weights to swing wildly. Ridge stabilizes them.

exercises.prompt: Which of these weight assignments for two highly correlated features is typical of a stabilized Ridge regression model?
exercises.explain: Ridge shares the weight smoothly and keeps the values small, preventing them from fighting each other with massive opposite signs.

exercises.prompt: You are building a house-price predictor using both 'number of rooms' and 'total square footage'. Standard linear regression gives these features giant, opposing weights (+8,000 and -7,800) because they are highly correlated, causing wild predictions on test data. How does Ridge regression fix this?

options.text: It penalizes the squared weights, forcing the model to distribute small, positive weights (+0.4 and +0.3) smoothly between both features
It deletes the 'total square footage' feature entirely
It multiplies the target price by 10 to hide the variance
It forces both weights to be exactly equal to 1.0

exercises.explain: Spot on! Ridge prevents correlated features from 'fighting' with giant opposite weights, sharing the weight smoothly and keeping predictions stable. 🎉
